question:
7. Let \( w \) be an irrational number with \( 0<w<1 \). Prove that \( w \) has a unique convergent expansion of the form
\[
w=\frac{1}{p_{0}}-\frac{1}{p_{0} p_{1}}+\frac{1}{p_{0} p_{1} p_{2}}-\frac{1}{p_{0} p_{1} p_{2} p_{3}}+\cdots
\]
where \( p_{0}, p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots \) are integers and \( 1 \leq p_{0}<p_{1}<p_{2}<\cdots \). If \( w= \) \( \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), find \( p_{0}, p_{1}, p_{2} \).
solution:
Solution. Before starting the construction we make an observation.
(1) If \( \alpha \) is an irrational number such that \( 0<\alpha<1 \), there is a unique integer \( p \) such that
\[
\frac{1}{p+1}<\alpha<\frac{1}{p}
\]
moreover, \( p \) is positive, \( 1-p \alpha \) is irrational, and \( 0<1-p \alpha<1 /(p+1) \). Now define a sequence \( w_{0}, w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots \) of irrational numbers between 0 and 1 and a sequence of positive integers by induction as follows:

Let \( w_{0}=w \) and let \( p_{0} \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{p_{0}+1}<w_{0}<\frac{1}{p_{0}}
\]

Let \( w_{1}=1-p_{0} w_{0}( \) which is irrational and between 0 and 1 , by (1)) and let \( p_{1} \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{p_{1}+1}<w_{1}<\frac{1}{p_{1}}
\]

After \( \omega_{k} \), and \( p_{k} \), have been defined so that
\[
\frac{1}{p_{k}+1}<w_{k}:<\frac{1}{p_{k},}
\]
let \( w_{k}=1-p_{k} \omega_{k} 1 \), which is irrational and between 0 and 1 , and let \( p_{k} \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{p_{k}+1}<w_{k}<\frac{1}{p_{k}}
\]

From (1) and (2) it follows that
\[
n_{k}^{\prime}<\frac{1}{p_{k}+1}
\]
so \( p_{k} \geq p_{k}+1 \). Hence the \( p \) 's increase strictly. Now
\[
\boldsymbol{w}_{k}=\frac{1}{p_{k}}-\frac{w_{k+1}}{p_{k}} \text { for } k=0,1,2, \ldots
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
w & =w_{0}=\frac{1}{p_{0}}-\frac{w_{1}}{p_{0}} \\
& =\frac{1}{p_{0}}-\frac{1}{p_{0} p_{1}}+\frac{w_{2}}{p_{0} p_{1}} \\
& =\sum_{n=0}^{k} \frac{(-1)^{n}}{p_{0} p_{1} \cdots p_{n}}+(-1)^{k+1} \frac{w_{k+1}}{p_{0} p_{1} \cdots p_{k}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( k \). The \( p \) 's increase strictly, so \( p_{0} p_{1} \cdots p_{k} \geq(k+1) \) ! Furthermore, the \( w \) 's are bounded, so the last term of (3) approaches 0 as \( k \rightarrow \infty \). Hence we have
\[
w=\sum_{n}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n}}{p_{0} p_{1} \cdots p_{n}}
\]
as required.
Next we show uniqueness. Suppose
\[
w=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n}}{q_{0} q_{1} \cdots q_{n}}
\]
where \( q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{2}, \ldots \) is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers. We shall prove that, if the preceding construction is carried out, \( p_{n}=q_{n} \) for all \( n \).

Since the series appearing in (4) is alternating with terms strictly decreasing in absolute value, we have
\[
\frac{1}{q_{0}}>w_{0}>\frac{1}{q_{0}}-\frac{1}{q_{0} q_{1}} \geq \frac{1}{q_{0}}-\frac{1}{q_{0}\left(q_{0}+1\right)}=\frac{1}{q_{0}+1}
\]

Therefore \( p_{0}=q_{0} \), and
\[
w_{1}=1-p_{0} w_{0}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{q_{1} q_{2} \cdots q_{n}}
\]

Repeating this argument inductively we find \( p_{k-1}=q_{k-1} \) and
\[
w_{k}=\sum_{n=k}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n-k}}{q_{k} q_{k+1} \cdots q_{n}}
\]
for \( k=1,2,3, \ldots \) This proves the uniqueness of the expansion.
If \( \boldsymbol{w}_{0}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), then \( p_{0}=1 \).
\[
\begin{array}{c}
w_{1}=1-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}=\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{2}}, \text { so } p_{1}=[2+\sqrt{2}]=3 \\
w_{2}=1-3\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4+3 \sqrt{2}}, \quad \text { so } p_{2}=[4+3 \sqrt{2}]=8
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If we start with a rational number \( \boldsymbol{w} \) in the interval \( (0,1) \), we are led to a terminating expansion of the form (4). The expansion is not unique, however; for example,
\[
\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1 \cdot 3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1 \cdot 2}+\frac{1}{1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3}
\]

The series (4) will converge for any strictly increasing sequence of positive integers \( \left\{q_{i}\right\} \), and it is easy to see that the sum will always be irrational. Hence
\[
w_{0} \mapsto\left\{p_{0}, p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots\right\}
\]
is an explicit bijective correspondence between the irrational numbers in \( (0,1) \) and the strictly increasing sequences of positive integers.

descriptive_long.question:
7. Let \( irration \) be an irrational number with \( 0<irration<1 \). Prove that \( irration \) has a unique convergent expansion of the form
\[
irration=\frac{1}{intpzero}-\frac{1}{intpzero intpone}+\frac{1}{intpzero intpone intptwo}-\frac{1}{intpzero intpone intptwo p_{3}}+\cdots
\]
where \( intpzero, intpone, intptwo, \ldots \) are integers and \( 1 \leq intpzero<intpone<intptwo<\cdots \). If \( irration= \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), find \( intpzero, intpone, intptwo \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Before starting the construction we make an observation.
(1) If \( alphavar \) is an irrational number such that \( 0<alphavar<1 \), there is a unique integer \( intpvar \) such that
\[
\frac{1}{intpvar+1}<alphavar<\frac{1}{intpvar}
\]
moreover, \( intpvar \) is positive, \( 1-intpvar\,alphavar \) is irrational, and \( 0<1-intpvar\,alphavar<1 /(intpvar+1) \). Now define a sequence \( irrzeroth, irrfirst, irrsecond, \ldots \) of irrational numbers between 0 and 1 and a sequence of positive integers by induction as follows:

Let \( irrzeroth=irration \) and let \( intpzero \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{intpzero+1}<irrzeroth<\frac{1}{intpzero}
\]

Let \( irrfirst=1-intpzero\,irrzeroth \) (which is irrational and between 0 and 1, by (1)) and let \( intpone \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{intpone+1}<irrfirst<\frac{1}{intpone}
\]

After \( omegaterm \) and \( intpkth \) have been defined so that
\[
\frac{1}{intpkth+1}<irrkth<\frac{1}{intpkth},
\]
let \( irrkth=1-intpkth\,omegaterm \), which is irrational and between 0 and 1, and let \( intpkth \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{intpkth+1}<irrkth<\frac{1}{intpkth}
\]

From (1) and (2) it follows that
\[
indexnk^{\prime}<\frac{1}{intpkth+1}
\]
so \( intpkth \ge intpkth+1 \). Hence the \( intpvar \)'s increase strictly. Now
\[
\boldsymbol{irrkth}=\frac{1}{intpkth}-\frac{w_{k+1}}{intpkth}\quad\text{for}\quad indexk=0,1,2,\ldots
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
irration &= irrzeroth = \frac{1}{intpzero}-\frac{irrfirst}{intpzero} \\
&=\frac{1}{intpzero}-\frac{1}{intpzero intpone}+\frac{irrsecond}{intpzero intpone} \\
&=\sum_{indexn=0}^{indexk} \frac{(-1)^{indexn}}{intpzero intpone \cdots intp_{n}}+(-1)^{indexk+1}\frac{w_{k+1}}{intpzero intpone \cdots intpkth}
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( indexk \). The \( intpvar \)'s increase strictly, so \( intpzero intpone \cdots intpkth \ge (indexk+1)! \). Furthermore, the \( irration \)'s are bounded, so the last term of (3) approaches 0 as \( indexk \to \infty \). Hence we have
\[
irration=\sum_{indexn}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{indexn}}{intpzero intpone \cdots intp_{n}}
\]
as required.

Next we show uniqueness. Suppose
\[
irration=\sum_{indexn=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{indexn}}{intqzero intqone \cdots q_{n}}
\]
where \( intqzero, intqone, intqtwo, \ldots \) is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers. We shall prove that, if the preceding construction is carried out, \( p_{n}=q_{n} \) for all \( indexn \).

Since the series appearing in (4) is alternating with terms strictly decreasing in absolute value, we have
\[
\frac{1}{intqzero}>irrzeroth>\frac{1}{intqzero}-\frac{1}{intqzero intqone}\ge\frac{1}{intqzero}-\frac{1}{intqzero(intqzero+1)}=\frac{1}{intqzero+1}
\]

Therefore \( intpzero=intqzero \), and
\[
irrfirst=1-intpzero\,irrzeroth=\sum_{indexn=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{indexn-1}}{intqone intqtwo \cdots q_{n}}
\]

Repeating this argument inductively we find \( p_{k-1}=q_{k-1} \) and
\[
irrkth=\sum_{indexn=indexk}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{indexn-indexk}}{intqkth q_{k+1} \cdots q_{n}}
\]
for \( indexk=1,2,3,\ldots \). This proves the uniqueness of the expansion.

If \( \boldsymbol{irrzeroth}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), then \( intpzero=1 \).
\[
\begin{array}{c}
irrfirst=1-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2}=\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{2}},\quad\text{so}\; intpone=[2+\sqrt{2}]=3 \\
irrsecond=1-3\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4+3\sqrt{2}},\quad\text{so}\; intptwo=[4+3\sqrt{2}]=8
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If we start with a rational number \( \boldsymbol{irration} \) in the interval \( (0,1) \), we are led to a terminating expansion of the form (4). The expansion is not unique, however; for example,
\[
\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1\cdot3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1\cdot2}+\frac{1}{1\cdot2\cdot3}
\]

The series (4) will converge for any strictly increasing sequence of positive integers \( \{intqith\} \), and it is easy to see that the sum will always be irrational. Hence
\[
irrzeroth \mapsto \{intpzero, intpone, intptwo, \ldots\}
\]
is an explicit bijective correspondence between the irrational numbers in \( (0,1) \) and the strictly increasing sequences of positive integers.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
7. Let \( dragonfruit \) be an irrational number with \( 0<dragonfruit<1 \). Prove that \( dragonfruit \) has a unique convergent expansion of the form
\[
dragonfruit=\frac{1}{sailboats}-\frac{1}{sailboats starships}+\frac{1}{sailboats starships raincloud}-\frac{1}{sailboats starships raincloud p_{3}}+\cdots
\]
where \( sailboats, starships, raincloud, \ldots \) are integers and \( 1 \leq sailboats<starships<raincloud<\cdots \). If \( dragonfruit= \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), find \( sailboats, starships, raincloud \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Before starting the construction we make an observation.
(1) If \( hazelnuts \) is an irrational number such that \( 0<hazelnuts<1 \), there is a unique integer \( lightning \) such that
\[
\frac{1}{lightning+1}<hazelnuts<\frac{1}{lightning}
\]
moreover, \( lightning \) is positive, \( 1-lightning\,hazelnuts \) is irrational, and \( 0<1-lightning\,hazelnuts<1 /(lightning+1) \). Now define a sequence \( elderberry, butterscotch, pomegranate, \ldots \) of irrational numbers between 0 and 1 and a sequence of positive integers by induction as follows:

Let \( elderberry=dragonfruit \) and let \( sailboats \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{sailboats+1}<elderberry<\frac{1}{sailboats}
\]

Let \( butterscotch=1-sailboats\,elderberry \) (which is irrational and between 0 and 1, by (1)) and let \( starships \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{starships+1}<butterscotch<\frac{1}{starships}
\]

After \( marshmallow \) and \( sandcastle \) have been defined so that
\[
\frac{1}{sandcastle+1}<butternut<\frac{1}{sandcastle},
\]
let \( butternut=1-sandcastle\,marshmallow \), which is irrational and between 0 and 1, and let \( sandcastle \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{sandcastle+1}<butternut<\frac{1}{sandcastle}
\]

From (1) and (2) it follows that
\[
fireflower^{\prime}<\frac{1}{sandcastle+1}
\]
so \( sandcastle \geq sandcastle+1 \). Hence the \( lightning \)'s increase strictly. Now
\[
\boldsymbol{butternut}=\frac{1}{sandcastle}-\frac{w_{thumbtacks+1}}{sandcastle} \text { for } thumbtacks=0,1,2,\ldots
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
dragonfruit & =elderberry=\frac{1}{sailboats}-\frac{butterscotch}{sailboats} \\
& =\frac{1}{sailboats}-\frac{1}{sailboats starships}+\frac{pomegranate}{sailboats starships} \\
& =\sum_{journeyman=0}^{thumbtacks} \frac{(-1)^{journeyman}}{sailboats starships \cdots sandcastle}+(-1)^{thumbtacks+1} \frac{w_{thumbtacks+1}}{sailboats starships \cdots sandcastle}
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( thumbtacks \). The \( lightning \)'s increase strictly, so \( sailboats starships \cdots sandcastle \geq(thumbtacks+1)! \) Furthermore, the \( dragonfruit \)'s are bounded, so the last term of (3) approaches 0 as \( thumbtacks \rightarrow \infty \). Hence we have
\[
dragonfruit=\sum_{journeyman=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{journeyman}}{sailboats starships \cdots p_{journeyman}}
\]
as required.

Next we show uniqueness. Suppose
\[
dragonfruit=\sum_{journeyman=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{journeyman}}{snowflake springtime \cdots q_{journeyman}}
\]
where \( snowflake, springtime, waterwheel, \ldots \) is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers. We shall prove that, if the preceding construction is carried out, \( sailboats=snowflake \) for all indices.

Since the series appearing in (4) is alternating with terms strictly decreasing in absolute value, we have
\[
\frac{1}{snowflake}>elderberry>\frac{1}{snowflake}-\frac{1}{snowflake springtime} \geq \frac{1}{snowflake}-\frac{1}{snowflake\left(snowflake+1\right)}=\frac{1}{snowflake+1}
\]

Therefore \( sailboats=snowflake \), and
\[
butterscotch=1-sailboats\,elderberry=\sum_{journeyman=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{journeyman-1}}{springtime waterwheel \cdots q_{journeyman}}
\]

Repeating this argument inductively we find \( sandcastle=gemstone \) and
\[
butternut=\sum_{journeyman=thumbtacks}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{journeyman-thumbtacks}}{gemstone q_{thumbtacks+1} \cdots q_{journeyman}}
\]
for \( thumbtacks=1,2,3,\ldots \). This proves the uniqueness of the expansion.

If \( \boldsymbol{elderberry}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), then \( sailboats=1 \).
\[
\begin{array}{c}
butterscotch=1-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}=\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{2}}, \text { so } starships=[2+\sqrt{2}]=3 \\
pomegranate=1-3\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4+3 \sqrt{2}}, \quad \text { so } raincloud=[4+3 \sqrt{2}]=8
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If we start with a rational number \( \boldsymbol{dragonfruit} \) in the interval \( (0,1) \), we are led to a terminating expansion of the form (4). The expansion is not unique, however; for example,
\[
\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1 \cdot 3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1 \cdot 2}+\frac{1}{1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3}
\]

The series (4) will converge for any strictly increasing sequence of positive integers \( \left\{honeycomb\right\} \), and it is easy to see that the sum will always be irrational. Hence
\[
elderberry \mapsto\left\{sailboats, starships, raincloud, \ldots\right\}
\]
is an explicit bijective correspondence between the irrational numbers in \( (0,1) \) and the strictly increasing sequences of positive integers.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
7. Let \( rationalvalue \) be an irrational number with \( 0<rationalvalue<1 \). Prove that \( rationalvalue \) has a unique convergent expansion of the form
\[
rationalvalue=\frac{1}{fractionzero}-\frac{1}{fractionzero fractionone}+\frac{1}{fractionzero fractionone fractiontwo}-\frac{1}{fractionzero fractionone fractiontwo fractionpart_{3}}+\cdots
\]
where \( fractionzero, fractionone, fractiontwo, \ldots \) are integers and \( 1 \leq fractionzero<fractionone<fractiontwo<\cdots \). If \( rationalvalue= \) \( \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{2} \), find \( fractionzero, fractionone, fractiontwo \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Before starting the construction we make an observation.

(1) If \( rationalalpha \) is an irrational number such that \( 0<rationalalpha<1 \), there is a unique integer \( fractionpart \) such that
\[
\frac{1}{fractionpart+1}<rationalalpha<\frac{1}{fractionpart}
\]
moreover, \( fractionpart \) is positive, \( 1-fractionpart\,rationalalpha \) is irrational, and \( 0<1-fractionpart\,rationalalpha<\frac{1}{fractionpart+1} \).

Now define a sequence \( rationalzero, rationalone, rationaltwo, \ldots \) of irrational numbers between 0 and 1 and a sequence of positive integers by induction as follows:

Let \( rationalzero=rationalvalue \) and let \( fractionzero \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{fractionzero+1}<rationalzero<\frac{1}{fractionzero}
\]

Let \( rationalone=1-fractionzero\,rationalzero \) (which is irrational and between 0 and 1, by (1)) and let \( fractionone \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{fractionone+1}<rationalone<\frac{1}{fractionone}
\]

After \( steadysequence \) and \( fractionk \) have been defined so that
\[
\frac{1}{fractionk+1}<rationalkappa<\frac{1}{fractionk},
\]
let \( rationalkappa=1-fractionk\,steadysequence \), which is irrational and between 0 and 1, and let \( fractionk \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{fractionk+1}<rationalkappa<\frac{1}{fractionk}.
\]

From (1) and (2) it follows that
\[
nonindexk^{\prime}<\frac{1}{fractionk+1},
\]
so \( fractionk \geq fractionk+1 \). Hence the \( fractionpart \)'s increase strictly. Now
\[
\boldsymbol{rationalkappa}= \frac{1}{fractionk}-\frac{rationalvalue_{finishindex+1}}{fractionk} \quad\text{for } finishindex=0,1,2,\ldots
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
rationalvalue &= rationalzero = \frac{1}{fractionzero}-\frac{rationalone}{fractionzero} \\
&= \frac{1}{fractionzero}-\frac{1}{fractionzero\,fractionone}+\frac{rationaltwo}{fractionzero\,fractionone} \\
&= \sum_{nonindex=0}^{finishindex} \frac{(-1)^{nonindex}}{fractionzero\,fractionone\cdots fraction_{nonindex}}
      + (-1)^{finishindex+1}\frac{rationalvalue_{finishindex+1}}
      {fractionzero\,fractionone\cdots fractionk}.
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( finishindex \). The \( fractionpart \)'s increase strictly, so \( fractionzero\,fractionone\cdots fractionk \geq (finishindex+1)! \). Furthermore, the \( rationalvalue \)'s are bounded, so the last term of (3) approaches 0 as \( finishindex \rightarrow \infty \). Hence we have
\[
rationalvalue=\sum_{nonindex=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{nonindex}}{fractionzero\,fractionone\cdots fraction_{nonindex}},
\]
as required.

Next we show uniqueness. Suppose
\[
rationalvalue=\sum_{nonindex=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{nonindex}}{decimalzero\,decimalone\cdots decimal_{nonindex}},
\]
where \( decimalzero, decimalone, decimaltwo, \ldots \) is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers. We shall prove that, if the preceding construction is carried out, \( fraction_{nonindex}=decimal_{nonindex} \) for all \( nonindex \).

Since the series appearing in (4) is alternating with terms strictly decreasing in absolute value, we have
\[
\frac{1}{decimalzero}>rationalzero>\frac{1}{decimalzero}-\frac{1}{decimalzero\,decimalone}
      \geq \frac{1}{decimalzero}-\frac{1}{decimalzero\bigl(decimalzero+1\bigr)}
      =\frac{1}{decimalzero+1}.
\]

Therefore \( fractionzero=decimalzero \), and
\[
rationalone = 1-fractionzero\,rationalzero
            = \sum_{nonindex=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{nonindex-1}}
              {decimalone\,decimaltwo\cdots decimal_{nonindex}}.
\]

Repeating this argument inductively we find \( fraction_{finishindex-1}=decimal_{finishindex-1} \) and
\[
rationalkappa = \sum_{nonindex=finishindex}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{nonindex-finishindex}}
               {decimal_{finishindex}\,decimal_{finishindex+1}\cdots decimal_{nonindex}}
\]
for \( finishindex=1,2,3,\ldots \). This proves the uniqueness of the expansion.

If \( \boldsymbol{rationalzero}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2} \), then \( fractionzero=1 \).
\[
\begin{array}{c}
rationalone = 1-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2} = \frac{1}{2+\sqrt{2}}, \quad\text{so } fractionone=[2+\sqrt{2}]=3,\\
rationaltwo = 1-3\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4+3\sqrt{2}}, \quad\text{so } fractiontwo=[4+3\sqrt{2}]=8.
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If we start with a rational number \( \boldsymbol{rationalvalue} \) in the interval \( (0,1) \), we are led to a terminating expansion of the form (4). The expansion is not unique, however; for example,
\[
\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1\cdot3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1\cdot2}+\frac{1}{1\cdot2\cdot3}.
\]

The series (4) will converge for any strictly increasing sequence of positive integers \( \{decimalindex\} \), and it is easy to see that the sum will always be irrational. Hence
\[
rationalzero \mapsto \{fractionzero, fractionone, fractiontwo, \ldots\}
\]
is an explicit bijective correspondence between the irrational numbers in \( (0,1) \) and the strictly increasing sequences of positive integers.

garbled_string.question:
7. Let \( bntvxclm \) be an irrational number with \( 0<bntvxclm<1 \). Prove that \( bntvxclm \) has a unique convergent expansion of the form
\[
bntvxclm=\frac{1}{lkhqszpo}-\frac{1}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg}+\frac{1}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg\, qbnvlxsa}-\frac{1}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg\, qbnvlxsa\, jscvhrmd_{3}}+\cdots
\]
where \( lkhqszpo, mzdfqubg, qbnvlxsa, \ldots \) are integers and \( 1 \le lkhqszpo<mzdfqubg<qbnvlxsa<\cdots \). If \( bntvxclm=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2} \), find \( lkhqszpo, mzdfqubg, qbnvlxsa \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Before starting the construction we make an observation.

(1) If \( qzxwvtnp \) is an irrational number such that \( 0<qzxwvtnp<1 \), there is a unique integer \( jscvhrmd \) such that
\[
\frac{1}{jscvhrmd+1}<qzxwvtnp<\frac{1}{jscvhrmd}
\]
moreover, \( jscvhrmd \) is positive, \( 1-jscvhrmd\, qzxwvtnp \) is irrational, and
\( 0<1-jscvhrmd\, qzxwvtnp<\frac{1}{jscvhrmd+1} \).

Now define a sequence \( gkqpsdva, mlzfhwre, pdxtkqbn, \ldots \) of irrational numbers between 0 and 1 and a sequence of positive integers by induction as follows:

Let \( gkqpsdva=bntvxclm \) and let \( lkhqszpo \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{lkhqszpo+1}<gkqpsdva<\frac{1}{lkhqszpo}
\]

Let \( mlzfhwre=1-lkhqszpo\, gkqpsdva \) (which is irrational and between 0 and 1, by (1)) and let \( mzdfqubg \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{mzdfqubg+1}<mlzfhwre<\frac{1}{mzdfqubg}
\]

After \( hjgrksla \) and \( hvdrlgpe \) have been defined so that
\[
\frac{1}{hvdrlgpe+1}<vrplsgae<\frac{1}{hvdrlgpe},
\]
let
\( vrplsgae=1-hvdrlgpe\, hjgrksla \),
which is irrational and between 0 and 1, and let \( hvdrlgpe \) be the integer such that
\[
\frac{1}{hvdrlgpe+1}<vrplsgae<\frac{1}{hvdrlgpe}
\]

From (1) and (2) it follows that
\[
 tpxwgnob^{\prime}<\frac{1}{hvdrlgpe+1}
\]
so \( hvdrlgpe\ge hvdrlgpe+1 \). Hence the \( jscvhrmd \)'s increase strictly. Now
\[
\boldsymbol{vrplsgae}=\frac{1}{hvdrlgpe}-\frac{bntvxclm_{rfzhykua+1}}{hvdrlgpe}\quad\text{for } rfzhykua=0,1,2,\ldots
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
bntvxclm &= gkqpsdva=\frac{1}{lkhqszpo}-\frac{mlzfhwre}{lkhqszpo}\\
&=\frac{1}{lkhqszpo}-\frac{1}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg}+\frac{pdxtkqbn}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg}\\
&=\sum_{sldmtqpa=0}^{rfzhykua}\frac{(-1)^{sldmtqpa}}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg\cdots jscvhrmd_{sldmtqpa}}
+(-1)^{rfzhykua+1}\frac{bntvxclm_{rfzhykua+1}}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg\cdots jscvhrmd_{rfzhykua}}
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( rfzhykua \). The \( jscvhrmd \)'s increase strictly, so
\( lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg\cdots jscvhrmd_{rfzhykua}\ge (rfzhykua+1)! \).
Furthermore, the \( bntvxclm \)'s are bounded, so the last term of (3) approaches 0 as \( rfzhykua\rightarrow\infty \). Hence we have
\[
bntvxclm=\sum_{sldmtqpa=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{sldmtqpa}}{lkhqszpo\, mzdfqubg\cdots jscvhrmd_{sldmtqpa}}
\]
as required.

Next we show uniqueness. Suppose
\[
bntvxclm=\sum_{sldmtqpa=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{sldmtqpa}}{czvalrje\, knshvtep\cdots ywzrmcob_{sldmtqpa}}
\]
where \( czvalrje, knshvtep, pvcluqsm, \ldots \) is a strictly increasing sequence of positive integers. We shall prove that, if the preceding construction is carried out, \( jscvhrmd_{sldmtqpa}=ywzrmcob_{sldmtqpa} \) for all \( sldmtqpa \).

Since the series appearing in (4) is alternating with terms strictly decreasing in absolute value, we have
\[
\frac{1}{czvalrje}>gkqpsdva>\frac{1}{czvalrje}-\frac{1}{czvalrje\, knshvtep}
\ge \frac{1}{czvalrje}-\frac{1}{czvalrje\left(czvalrje+1\right)}=\frac{1}{czvalrje+1}
\]

Therefore \( lkhqszpo=czvalrje \), and
\[
mlzfhwre=1-lkhqszpo\, gkqpsdva=\sum_{sldmtqpa=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{sldmtqpa-1}}{knshvtep\, pvcluqsm\cdots ywzrmcob_{sldmtqpa}}
\]

Repeating this argument inductively we find \( jscvhrmd_{rfzhykua-1}=ywzrmcob_{rfzhykua-1} \) and
\[
vrplsgae=\sum_{sldmtqpa=rfzhykua}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{sldmtqpa-rfzhykua}}{ywzrmcob_{rfzhykua}\, ywzrmcob_{rfzhykua+1}\cdots ywzrmcob_{sldmtqpa}}
\]
for \( rfzhykua=1,2,3,\ldots \). This proves the uniqueness of the expansion.

If \( \boldsymbol{gkqpsdva}=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2} \), then \( lkhqszpo=1 \).
\[
\begin{array}{c}
mlzfhwre=1-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2}=\frac{1}{2+\sqrt{2}},\quad\text{so } mzdfqubg=[2+\sqrt{2}]=3\\[6pt]
pdxtkqbn=1-3\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4+3\sqrt{2}},\quad\text{so } qbnvlxsa=[4+3\sqrt{2}]=8
\end{array}
\]

Remark. If we start with a rational number \( \boldsymbol{bntvxclm} \) in the interval \( (0,1) \), we are led to a terminating expansion of the form (4). The expansion is not unique, however; for example,
\[
\frac{2}{3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1\cdot 3}=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1\cdot 2}+\frac{1}{1\cdot 2\cdot 3}
\]

The series (4) will converge for any strictly increasing sequence of positive integers \( \{sjtrmoxa\} \), and it is easy to see that the sum will always be irrational. Hence
\[
gkqpsdva \mapsto \{lkhqszpo, mzdfqubg, qbnvlxsa, \ldots\}
\]
is an explicit bijective correspondence between the irrational numbers in \( (0,1) \) and the strictly increasing sequences of positive integers.

kernel_variant.question:
Let \(w\) be an irrational real number with \(0<w<1\).  
(a)  Show that there is a unique strictly increasing sequence of positive integers
\[1\le p_0<p_1<p_2<\dots\]
such that the alternating infinite product-denominator series
\[
 w\;=\;\frac{1}{p_{0}}-\frac{1}{p_{0}p_{1}}+\frac{1}{p_{0}p_{1}p_{2}}-\frac{1}{p_{0}p_{1}p_{2}p_{3}}+\cdots
\]
converges to \(w\).

(b)  For the specific irrational
\[\displaystyle w=\pi-3\,(\approx0.14159),\]
compute the first three integers of the sequence, \(p_{0},p_{1},p_{2}.\)
kernel_variant.solution:
Part (a): Existence of the expansion
------------------------------------------------
Step 1  (unique integer bracketing).
For an irrational \(\alpha\in(0,1)\) there is a unique positive integer \(p\) satisfying
\[
\frac1{p+1}<\alpha<\frac1p.
\]
Indeed, \(p=\lfloor 1/\alpha\rfloor\) works and uniqueness is obvious.

Step 2  (recursive construction).
Put \(w_0=w\).  Having defined an irrational \(w_k\in(0,1)\), choose the unique
\(p_k\) with  \(1/(p_k+1)<w_k<1/p_k\) and set
\[
w_{k+1}=1-p_k w_k.
\]
Because \(w_k<1/p_k\) we get \(0<w_{k+1}<1\); irrationality of \(w_{k+1}\) follows
from that of \(w_k\).  Moreover
\[
w_{k+1}<\frac{1}{p_k+1}\ \Longrightarrow\ p_{k+1}>p_k,
\]
so the \(p_k\) form a strictly increasing sequence.

Step 3  (telescoping identity).
From the definition we have
\[
w_k = \frac1{p_k}-\frac{w_{k+1}}{p_k}\quad(k\ge0).
\]
Iterating this identity \(k+1\) times gives
\[
w = w_0 = \sum_{n=0}^{k} \frac{(-1)^n}{p_0p_1\dots p_n}
         +(-1)^{k+1}\,\frac{w_{k+1}}{p_0p_1\dots p_k}.
\]

Step 4  (convergence of the tail).
Since every \(w_{k+1}\) lies in \((0,1)\), the remainder has absolute value
\[
\Bigl|(-1)^{k+1}\,\frac{w_{k+1}}{p_0\dots p_k}\Bigr|<\frac1{p_0p_1\dots p_k}.
\]
Because \(p_1,p_2,\dots\) are all \(\ge2\) we have the easy bound
\[
p_0p_1\dots p_k\;\ge\;2^{\,k}\quad(k\ge1),
\]
so the denominator tends to infinity and the remainder tends to 0.  Hence the
infinite series converges to \(w\), proving existence of the desired expansion.

Part (b): Uniqueness of the sequence
------------------------------------
Suppose another strictly increasing sequence \(q_0<q_1<\dots\) satisfied the
same equality.  The resulting series is alternating with strictly decreasing
terms, so its first two partial sums give the bounds
\[
\frac1{q_0}>w>\frac1{q_0}-\frac1{q_0q_1}>\frac1{q_0+1}.
\]
By the uniqueness in Step 1 this forces \(p_0=q_0\).  Substituting back and
repeating the argument inductively yields \(p_n=q_n\) for all \(n\).  Hence the
sequence \((p_n))\) is unique.

Part (c): Numerical example  \(w=\pi-3\)
----------------------------------------
1.  First term.
\[
0.14159<\frac17\quad\text{and}\quad 0.14159>\frac18,\quad\text{so}\quad p_0=7.
\]

2.  Compute \(w_1\).
\[
w_1=1-7(\pi-3)=22-7\pi\approx0.008851425.
\]
Bracketing gives \(1/113< w_1 < 1/112\), hence \(p_1=112.\)

3.  Compute \(w_2\).
\[
w_2=1-112w_1=1-112(22-7\pi)=784\pi-2463\approx0.008640414.
\]
Now \(1/116< w_2 < 1/115\), so \(p_2=115.\)

Therefore the first three integers in the expansion of \(\pi-3\) are
\[
\boxed{p_0=7},\;\boxed{p_1=112},\;\boxed{p_2=115}.
\]

core_steps: Unique–integer bracketing: for any irrational α∈(0,1) there is exactly one p with 1/(p+1)<α<1/p.
Recursive construction: define w_{k+1}=1−p_k w_k and choose p_k via the bracket; this keeps w_k in (0,1) and forces p_{k+1}>p_k.
Telescoping identity: w_k = 1/p_k − w_{k+1}/p_k, iterated to express w as the alternating series Σ (−1)^n /(p_0…p_n) plus a tail.
Convergence/existence: product p_0…p_k grows unbounded (≥ (k+1)!) so the tail →0, giving the desired expansion.
Uniqueness: any other strictly increasing {q_n} gives an alternating series with decreasing terms; comparison of first–term bounds forces p_0=q_0 and inductively p_n=q_n.

slot1.description: Particular numerical example used to illustrate the construction and compute first few p_n.

slot2.description: Specific lower bound chosen for the product p_0 p_1 … p_k when proving convergence.
